Divyam Matia | Blog

DevOps notes from the infrastructure layer.

Practical writing on release engineering, cloud infrastructure, automation, Kubernetes, and DevSecOps - based on the systems I build and operate.

May 20, 2026 | 5 min read

From Manual Deployments to Reliable Release Engineering

Manual deployment work usually looks harmless until the same steps must be repeated under production pressure. A release engineer's job is to remove that hidden risk. In my work with Windows services and Kubernetes services, the biggest gains came from converting repeated release actions into predictable Azure DevOps pipelines.

Azure DevOpsRelease EngineeringCI/CD
May 20, 2026 | 6 min read

Automating Azure DevOps Project Setup with Ansible

Project setup becomes expensive when every new Azure DevOps environment depends on manual clicks: service connections, extensions, repositories, pipelines, permissions, and release definitions. Automation is the difference between "we remember how to do it" and "the platform knows how to do it."

AnsibleAzure DevOpsAutomation
May 20, 2026 | 6 min read

Terraform Pipelines for Safer Azure Infrastructure Changes

Infrastructure changes should not feel like editing production by hand. Terraform gives the language for infrastructure, but pipelines give the control system around it: validation, review, plan visibility, approval, and repeatable execution.

TerraformAzureIaC
May 20, 2026 | 5 min read

Designing DevSecOps Gates Without Slowing Teams Down

Security tools can either help engineers move with confidence or become noisy gates everyone tries to bypass. The difference is design. DevSecOps is most effective when checks are placed where feedback is fastest and most actionable.

DevSecOpsSecurityTesting
May 20, 2026 | 5 min read

Operating Kubernetes Services with Calm Infrastructure Habits

Kubernetes gives teams powerful deployment primitives, but calm operations come from the habits around the cluster. A service is only truly production-ready when deployment, monitoring, troubleshooting, and escalation paths are all understandable.

KubernetesObservabilityOperations
